pattern: Hexadecimal is base sixteen, so repeatedly taking the low four bits and mapping them to a hex character builds the digits from least to most significant; handling negatives as their 32-bit two's-complement bit pattern lets the same loop serve both signs.
hints: Each hex digit is exactly four bits, so masking the lowest four bits gives one digit and shifting right by four moves to the next.
A negative number is represented by its 32-bit two's complement pattern; adding two to the power thirty-two turns it into the equivalent non-negative bit pattern.
Repeatedly emit the hex character for value AND fifteen, then shift right by four, stopping when the value reaches zero; reverse the collected digits, and return a single zero for input zero.

edge_inputs.description: small positive needing a letter digit
negative one is all ones in 32 bits
zero is the single special case
a small negative exercises high-bit borrowing
exactly sixteen rolls into a two-digit result

stress.description: values spanning the full signed 32-bit range including both signs
stress.note: covers negatives that exercise the two's-complement path and positives alike
